original_abstract:
Urban Exploration (URBEX), the practice of investigating abandoned man-made structures and brownfields, has evolved from a niche subculture into a global phenomenon and a specialized form of non-consumption tourism. Despite its growing popularity, the legal relationship between the property owner and the explorer remains complex and under-researched. This paper examines the legal aspects of URBEX through the lens of the property owner within the framework of the Czech legal system. Utilizing a theoretical analysis of legislative sources, the study evaluates owner rights and obligations across three dimensions: civil, administrative, and criminal law. 

In the civil sphere, the research focuses on the "ownership entails obligations" principle, exploring the owner's right to protection against unauthorized entry via negatory actions, as well as their liability for damages resulting from building defects or neglect. From an administrative perspective, the paper details the statutory duties imposed by the Building Act and health and safety regulations, particularly regarding the maintenance and securing of brownfields and construction sites. Finally, the paper investigates potential criminal liability, hypothesizing how negligence-based offenses against life and health might apply to owners who fail to secure hazardous properties. Given the current lack of established judicial precedent in this specific area, this study provides a foundational theoretical framework for understanding the normative boundaries of property ownership in the age of modern urban exploration.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract Urban Exploration (URBEX) also referred to as the practice of abandoning and visiting abandoned man-made structures and brownfields is from the onset the act of a specific subculture. In the course of time, URBEX evolved into an individual and global phenomenon and it also belongs to non-consumption tourism. Therefore, it arises an unsolved question of the legal relationship between the property owner and the explorer and so it is unclearly determined. This paper deals with a solution of this issue based on the Czech law and focuses on interests of a property owner. The legislation regulating the rights and duties of the property owner in the field of Urban Exploration is derived from civil, administrative and criminal law, and therefore we carry out their theoretical comparison based on legislation.

As far as civil law is concerned, the focus was on the rule of dominus clausus (principle of ownership entails obligations) and on the negative and con sequences deriving from the negatory actions taken by the owner to safeguard his right not to be entered and to his liability in the event of accidents or damage caused by structural deficiencies or due to lack of maintenance. On the administrative level, the regulation by the Building Act and health and safety rules concerning the management and corresponding protection measures for brownfields and construction sites are described. As regards criminal law, a hypothetical assessment concerning the applicability of negligence-based criminal offences to life and health in the case of property owners failing to secure dangerous properties. At present there are no judicial decisions (case-law) that have set a definitive standard in relation to this field, and therefore this research fills a crucial knowledge gap by laying the foundations of a theory of the boundaries that must be respected by property rights in times of urban exploration.
